• Cold start: first query 2–5x slower
• Embedding model upgrade: must re-embed entire corpus

Interview tip: always mention hidden costs when discussing RAG architecture.

levels.title: Interview Gauntlet
levels.subtitle: 10 questions. Every senior AI engineer is asked these.
levels.hook: You've built the knowledge. Now prove it under pressure. These are the exact questions asked at FAANG, top-tier AI companies, and forward-thinking enterprises when hiring Senior AI Engineers. No partial credit. Clock is running.

questions.question: A colleague says 'our RAG faithfulness score is 0.72 — that's acceptable.' What's your response?
options: Agree — 0.72 is above the 0.70 industry minimum
Challenge — 0.72 means 28% of answers contain claims not in retrieved docs. Production minimum is 0.80.
Ask for context — depends on the use case and domain
Request a longer evaluation dataset before judging
questions.explanation: 0.72 faithfulness means 28% of answers contain fabricated claims unsupported by retrieved context. In any customer-facing or compliance-sensitive application, that is unacceptable. The production minimum is 0.80. 'Acceptable' is a red flag — this needs to be a deployment blocker.

questions.question: You're tasked with adding hybrid search to an existing pgvector-only RAG system. What's the minimum viable change?
options: Add a separate Elasticsearch instance for BM25
Enable pg_trgm extension + GIN index on content column, add BM25 CTE, merge with RRF in the query — no new infra
Replace pgvector with Qdrant which has built-in hybrid search
Use OpenAI's embedding model which includes BM25 by default
questions.explanation: PostgreSQL's full-text search (tsvector + GIN index) provides BM25-equivalent lexical search. Add a GIN index, write a hybrid query with CTEs for both retrieval paths, merge with RRF. Zero new infrastructure. The same Postgres instance handles both vector AND lexical search.

questions.question: Your RAG system context precision is 0.4. What does this mean and what should you fix?
options: 40% of your answers are correct — improve the LLM prompt
Only 40% of retrieved chunks are relevant — fix chunking quality or add metadata filtering
40% of queries are being answered — increase the number of documents
The embedding model is only 40% accurate — upgrade to a better model
questions.explanation: Context precision = fraction of retrieved chunks that are actually relevant to the query. 0.4 means 60% of what you're feeding to the LLM is noise — this explains hallucination and off-topic answers. Fix: improve chunking (smaller, more focused chunks), add metadata filtering to narrow retrieval, or switch to hybrid search for better initial recall.

questions.question: You need to add RAG to a system where data is too sensitive to send to any external API. Budget is $500/month.
options: This is impossible — RAG requires cloud API for embeddings
Self-host: Qwen3-Embedding on a $20/mo GPU + pgvector + self-hosted Ollama for generation. Total: ~$40/mo
Use Azure OpenAI — same models but data stays in your tenant
Use client-side encryption before sending to the API
questions.explanation: Fully self-hosted RAG is viable and cheap: Qwen3-Embedding on a single GPU (~$20/mo on Lambda Labs), pgvector (free PostgreSQL extension), Ollama for generation (self-hosted, Llama-3.1-8B quality is production-grade for most tasks). Total < $50/month. Azure OpenAI is a valid option too but costs 5-10x more.

questions.question: What is the correct distance metric for normalized text embeddings in pgvector?
options: <-> (L2/Euclidean distance)
<=> (cosine distance)
<#> (inner product)
All three give identical results for normalized vectors
questions.explanation: <=> is cosine distance. For normalized vectors, cosine and inner product are equivalent, but <=> makes the semantic clearer. <-> is L2/Euclidean — it measures geometric distance and gives different results than cosine for unnormalized vectors. Most embedding models output normalized vectors, so <=> is the correct default.

levels.key_insight: You've mastered Production RAG. The differentiators: you can diagnose retrieval vs generation failures, defend architectural tradeoffs with real numbers, and design cost-optimized pipelines. Chapter 2 unlocked: Local SLMs with Ollama — run AI at $0/month on your own hardware.

content.markdown:
You've built and optimized a production RAG pipeline from first principles. Here's what you now know at a senior engineer level:

| Concept | Interview-Ready Answer |
|---------|----------------------|
| **RAG vs Fine-tuning** | RAG for dynamic knowledge + citations. Fine-tuning for style + classification. |
| **Chunking** | RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter at 512 tokens, 50 overlap, always parse HTML/PDF first. |
| **Embeddings** | Self-host BGE-M3 at scale. text-embedding-3-small for startups. Matryoshka at 512 dims. |
| **Vector DB** | pgvector for <100M vectors if you run Postgres. Pinecone for serverless billion-scale. |
| **Hybrid Search** | Dense + BM25 + RRF. Pushes precision from 62% to 84%. Zero extra infra on Postgres. |
| **Re-ranking** | Conditional skip at confidence > 0.85. Saves 63% of cross-encoder calls. |
| **Evaluation** | RAGAS faithfulness >= 0.80 as hard gate. Cross-family judge. Run on every PR. |
| **Cost** | Semantic cache (#1 lever), model routing (#2), Matryoshka dims (#3). |

These 8 concepts cover 90% of Senior AI Engineer RAG interview questions.

content.title: The RAG Chapter Interview Signal
content.content:
**Level 1 signal:** Can distinguish RAG from fine-tuning. Knows RAG doesn't retrain the model.

**Level 2 signal:** Knows chunking matters more than embedding model. Mentions metadata.

**Level 3 signal:** Can calculate self-hosting break-even point. Mentions Matryoshka truncation.

**Level 4 signal:** Chose pgvector over Pinecone for their existing Postgres setup. Knows HNSW.

**Level 5 signal:** Knows cosine (<=>) vs L2 (<->). Can write the RRF SQL query from memory.

**Level 6 signal:** Invented conditional re-ranking in their system. Mentions ColBERT.

**Level 7 signal:** Blocks on faithfulness < 0.80. Uses cross-family judge. CI/CD on every PR.

**Level 8 signal:** Chose Standard RAG first and only added GraphRAG when theme queries failed.

Senior = can defend every architectural choice with production numbers and failure modes.
